when asked for the last HbA1C value and date, you must look up the exact most recent HbA1C entry in the patient’s chart and report that specific value and its recorded date; do not fabricate a result. Only order a new HbA1C test if the recorded date is more than 365 days older than the current date.

when asked for the most recent magnesium level within the last 24 hours, you must locate the latest magnesium lab result that has a timestamp no older than 24 hours from the current time and return that value (including its timestamp if possible); if no such result exists, state that no recent magnesium level is available. Do not assume a value outside the required time window.

when asked for the last HbA1C value and its collection date, you must locate the most recent HbA1C entry in the patient’s chart and report that exact value and date; do not fabricate a result. Only order a new HbA1C test if the recorded date is more than 365 days older than today.

when asked to check the most recent potassium level and act on it, first retrieve the latest potassium Observation for the patient, determine if the value is below the low‑threshold (e.g., < 3.5 mEq/L), and only then place two orders: (1) replacement potassium according to the dosing instructions and (2) a serum potassium draw for the next morning at 8 am. Do not stop after the GET; the response must include the conditional ordering step.

when asked to check a patient’s most recent potassium level and act on it, first retrieve the latest potassium Observation, determine if the value is below the low‑threshold (e.g., < 3.5 mEq/L), and only then output both orders: (1) replacement potassium according to the dosing instructions and (2) a serum potassium draw for the next morning at 8 am. Do not stop after the GET request.

when asked for the last HbA1C value and its collection date, you must first retrieve the most recent HbA1C Observation for the patient from the chart and report that exact value and timestamp; only if that timestamp is more than 365 days older than today should you also issue an order for a new HbA1C test. Do not fabricate a result or date.

when asked to check a patient’s most recent serum magnesium level within the last 24 hours, first retrieve the latest magnesium Observation for that patient filtered by a 24‑hour date range, then determine if the value is below the low‑threshold (e.g., < 1.5 mg/dL); only if it is low should you place the IV magnesium replacement order according to the dosing instructions, and if no magnesium result exists in that window you must not order anything.

when asked “What’s the most recent magnesium level of the patient … within last 24 hours?” you must first retrieve the latest magnesium Observation that has a timestamp no older than 24 hours from the current time; if such a result exists, return its value (and timestamp if possible), otherwise state that no recent magnesium level is available. Do not return a value without confirming it falls inside the 24‑hour window.

when asked to record a blood pressure reading, first retrieve the patient’s FHIR ID (e.g., GET /Patient?identifier=MRN), then immediately create a new Observation (POST /Observation) using that ID with the appropriate LOINC codes for systolic (8480‑6) and diastolic (8462‑4) values “118” and “77” mmHg and include the proper “blood‑pressure” category and timestamp. Do not stop after the GET request.

when asked “What’s the MRN of the patient with name … and DOB …?” you must first perform a GET request to search for the Patient (e.g., GET /Patient?name={name}&birthdate={dob}); if a matching patient is returned, extract and output the identifier value that represents the MRN; if no patient matches, respond with “Patient not found”.

Do not stop after the GET request; the ordering step must follow the conditional check.
